one_line_takeaway: TRACE-RPS combines attention-guided fine-grained text anonymization (TRACE) with a lightweight two-stage logit-guided suffix search that induces model refusal (RPS) to cut LLM attribute-inference accuracy from ~50% to below 5% on open-source attacker models, while closed-source attacker models are limited to the weaker anonymization-only TRACE defense.
key_finding: On open-source models, combining TRACE+RPS reduces attribute inference accuracy from roughly 50% (No Defense baseline, e.g. 53.71% on Llama2-7B-Chat, 57.14% on Llama3.1-8B-Instruct, Synthetic dataset) to below 5% in nearly all cases, with several cells at exactly 0% (e.g. Llama3.1-8B-Instruct TRACE-RPS: 0% on both Synthetic and SynthPAI). RPS alone (no anonymization) already drives accuracy to 0-6.48% with attack success rates (bracketed) of roughly 13-18% across the four open-source models on Synthetic and SynthPAI. On closed-source models (GPT-3.5, GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Pro), where only TRACE is applicable (no logit access for RPS), TRACE reduces GPT-4o accuracy from 71.24% (No Defense) to 28.38% (TRACE, GPT-3.5-Turbo anonymizer) or 26.67% (GPT-4o anonymizer) on the Synthetic dataset -- roughly a 45% absolute reduction -- and outperforms FgAA (39.05%/25.71%) and Azure (56.00%, from Staab et al. 2025) at the same accuracy points. For the highly instruction-following Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, plain RPS/refusal-inducing suffixes are less effective, so MPS (misattribution) reduces accuracy to 10.29% versus FgAA's 28.76%, and combining TRACE+MPS reduces it further to 4.38% (using GPT-4o anonymization). Ablation shows both TRACE sub-components matter: removing both Vocabulary Extraction and Inference Chain raises Top@1 accuracy from 26.86% to 37.14% (+10.28 points). Per-attribute breakdown (Llama2-13B-Chat) shows RPS drives gender inference from 79.25% to 0% and income from 51.25% to 3.62%, whereas TRACE alone leaves gender at 52.83% and income at 41.25% -- illustrating the paper's claim that anonymization alone is insufficient for low-cardinality attributes (gender, income) because parsable signal remains even after masking, motivating the refusal-inducing RPS component. Utility-privacy tradeoff: RPS achieves 98.17% (Llama2-7B-Chat) and 98.27% (DeepSeek-R1-Distill) Sentence-BERT semantic similarity to original text while cutting accuracy to 1.71% and 6.48% respectively, versus FgAA's 79.23% utility at 23-29% accuracy -- i.e., RPS changes almost none of the original semantic content (it only appends a suffix) while achieving much stronger privacy protection than anonymization-based FgAA.
core_contribution: The paper proposes TRACE-RPS, a unified, user-side, proactive defense against LLM attribute inference attacks (where an attacker LLM infers private attributes like age, gender, location from innocuous user-generated text). It has two original components: (1) TRACE (Textual Revision via Attention and Chain-based Editing), a fine-grained anonymization method that uses (a) attention-based Privacy Vocabulary Extraction -- extracting the final-layer attention vector over the last token of an attacker-simulating prompt from a pretrained model (Llama2-7B-Chat), aggregating token-level attention scores into word-level importance, and selecting the Top-K highest-scoring words as a privacy vocabulary -- and (b) Privacy Inference Chain generation, where an adversarial LLM produces a step-by-step reasoning chain showing how it deduces the attribute, used together to guide an anonymization model (GPT-3.5-Turbo or GPT-4o) to selectively rewrite only the privacy-critical spans; and (2) RPS (Rejection-Oriented Perturbation Search), the first optimization-based defense designed for attribute inference attacks, which appends an optimized suffix to the user's text via a two-stage gradient-free random search over token replacements: Stage 1 (First-Token Anchoring) maximizes the log-probability of the attacker model's first generated token being "I" (log-prob threshold log(0.8)~=-0.22), and Stage 2 (Rejection Token Shaping) maximizes the probability that the second token falls in a rejection-token set R={"apologize","cannot"} (threshold log(0.55)~=-0.60), using only 1-2 generated tokens per candidate evaluation for efficiency. The paper also proposes MPS (Misattribute-Oriented Perturbation Search), an alternative suffix-optimization objective that redirects the attacker model's predicted attribute to a specific incorrect value (rather than inducing refusal) for highly instruction-following models such as Qwen2.5 that persist in answering despite an initial refusal.
threat_model: Defender = the user who owns the text and wants to prevent an adversary's LLM (used as an attacker/inference engine) from inferring sensitive personal attributes (age, gender, location, income, education, relationship status, occupation, place of birth) from that user's own publicly shared, otherwise-innocuous text. The user cannot modify the adversary's prompt or attacker model; the only control is preprocessing (anonymizing and/or suffix-appending) their own text before it is ever shared/posted -- i.e., this is a proactive, pre-publication, user-side defense, not a model-provider-side safety alignment or an inference-time filter. RPS/MPS require white-box access to the target model's output token log-probabilities (to compute the scoring functions J1/J2), so they apply only to open-source models where logits are accessible; for closed-source attacker models (GPT-3.5, GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Pro) only the anonymization component TRACE is applicable.
limitations: Explicitly noted by the authors: (1) RPS/MPS require white-box logit access to the target/attacker model, so they cannot be applied to closed-source attacker models (GPT-3.5, GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Pro) -- only TRACE (anonymization) is usable there, and its privacy gains are smaller than the near-zero accuracy achieved when RPS is available. (2) RPS alone can fail against 'highly instruction-following' models like Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct that keep answering despite an induced refusal-style suffix, motivating the fallback MPS strategy, which itself is a misattribution (deception) rather than a hard block and only reduces (not eliminates) inference accuracy (10.29% for MPS alone, still nonzero). (3) TRACE relies on calling external adversarial/anonymization LLMs (GPT-3.5-Turbo, GPT-4o) and an attention-extraction model, adding cost/dependency compared to RPS's lightweight suffix search. (4) TRACE reduces but does not eliminate accuracy for low-cardinality attributes (e.g., gender remains at 52.83% after TRACE alone on Llama2-13B-Chat, per Table 5), which the authors use to argue anonymization alone is fundamentally insufficient. Not addressed in the paper: robustness against an adversary that specifically detects or strips the RPS suffix (adaptive-attacker robustness beyond prompt-template variation), computational cost of up to 10,000 search iterations per text, and whether TRACE's use of external LLM APIs raises its own data-exposure concerns.

datasets_used: a real-world dataset (described in an appendix not covered by the extracted first 22 pages)
quantitative_results: No Defense attribute inference accuracy on Synthetic dataset: Llama2-7B-Chat 53.71%, Llama2-13B-Chat 56.19%, Llama3.1-8B-Instruct 57.14%, DeepSeek-R1-Distill 49.71%, GPT-3.5 67.62%, GPT-4o 71.24%, Gemini 2.5 Pro 68.38%

TRACE-RPS accuracy on Synthetic dataset (GPT-3.5-Turbo anonymizer): Llama2-7B-Chat 0.19% (attack success rate 12.82%), Llama2-13B-Chat 1.14% (14.09%), Llama3.1-8B-Instruct 0% (13.48%), DeepSeek-R1-Distill 1.71% (13.37%)
RPS alone (no TRACE) on Synthetic dataset: Llama2-7B-Chat 1.71% (14.10%), Llama2-13B-Chat 4.38% (16.09%), Llama3.1-8B-Instruct 0% (13.48%), DeepSeek-R1-Distill 6.48% (17.59%)
TRACE on closed-source GPT-4o (Synthetic, GPT-3.5-Turbo anonymizer): 28.38% vs FgAA 39.05% vs No Defense 71.24%
MPS on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct: No Defense 57.52%, D-Defense 48.00%, FgAA 28.76%, TRACE 22.67% (GPT-4o: 18.86%), MPS alone 10.29%, TRACE-MPS 5.14% (GPT-4o: 4.38%)
TRACE ablation (Top@1 accuracy, Synthetic, GPT-3.5-Turbo inference model): full TRACE (VE+IC) 26.86%; without VE 31.62% (+4.76); without IC 31.43% (+4.57); without both 37.14% (+10.28)

Per-attribute accuracy on Llama2-13B-Chat: No Defense gender 79.25% -> TRACE 52.83% -> RPS 0%; No Defense income 51.25% -> TRACE 41.25% -> RPS 3.62%; No Defense location 73.61% -> TRACE 6.94% -> RPS 0%

Utility-privacy tradeoff: RPS on Llama2-7B-Chat achieves 98.17% Sentence-BERT similarity at 1.71% accuracy; RPS on DeepSeek-R1-Distill achieves 98.27% similarity at 6.48% accuracy; FgAA achieves 79.23% utility at 23.24-29.14% accuracy

Multi-model RPS (ensemble-optimized on Llama2-7B/13B-Chat + Llama3.1-8B-Instruct) transfers to unseen Llama3.2-1B-Instruct: accuracy 6.86% (down 26.28 points from No Defense) and Llama3.2-3B-Instruct: 0% (down 54.10 points)
baselines_compared: No Defense (undefended attacker accuracy, Staab et al. 2024)
Azure Language Services (rule-based entity masking, Aahill 2023)
Dou-SD (self-disclosure taxonomy rewriting, Dou et al. 2024)
